journal_entry.description: Declaration of cash dividend payable in 30 days
journal_entry.rationale: Under IFRS, when a cash dividend is declared by the board, it becomes a legal obligation. The debit increases Dividends (or Retained Earnings under some practices), and the credit creates a liability (Dividends Payable) until payment is made. The tuples indicate b=1 for dividend increase (debit) and a=1 for liability increase (credit), matching the declaration event. No tax applies to dividend declarations in Canada.

output.reason: When a dividend is declared, the debit side records a dividend increase (owner distribution). The declaration creates a liability (Dividends Payable) on the credit side, but the debit side captures the dividend itself. This is 1 debit line increasing dividends.

debit_classifier.reason: When a dividend is declared, the debit side records a dividend increase (owner distribution). The declaration creates a liability (Dividends Payable) on the credit side, but the debit side captures the dividend itself. This is 1 debit line increasing dividends.

output.reason: When a dividend is declared but not yet paid, a liability (Dividends Payable) is created. This is a liability increase, which is credited. The debit side (not classified here) would be to Dividends/Retained Earnings. The actual cash payment will occur later when the dividend is paid in 30 days.

credit_classifier.reason: When a dividend is declared but not yet paid, a liability (Dividends Payable) is created. This is a liability increase, which is credited. The debit side (not classified here) would be to Dividends/Retained Earnings. The actual cash payment will occur later when the dividend is paid in 30 days.
